authoritative_sources.title: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST publishes the national cybersecurity framework used across industries to organize governance, risk management, and cybersecurity outcomes.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: governance intake, scope framing, and risk context

authoritative_sources.title: Risk Management Framework for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST RMF is the primary public risk-management workflow reference for prepare, categorize, select, implement, assess, authorize, and monitor activities.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: prerequisite validation, authorization boundary, and monitoring handoff

authoritative_sources.title: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 provides a public control catalog with program management, planning, assessment, authorization, and privacy controls used for policy mapping and evidence definitions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control mapping and evidence checklist creation

authoritative_sources.title: Managing Information Security Risk
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-39 defines organizational, mission/business-process, and system-level risk management concepts that constrain policy prioritization and residual-risk routing.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: risk prioritization and residual-risk owner routing

authoritative_sources.title: Cross-Sector Cybersecurity Performance Goals
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA publishes cross-sector goals to help critical infrastructure and smaller organizations prioritize baseline cybersecurity practices.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cross-sector policy prerequisite and priority framing

authoritative_sources.title: Cyber Resilience Review
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA CRR is an official assessment service and workflow reference for cybersecurity management practices and resilience maturity.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: resilience governance assessment and evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: Zero Trust Maturity Model Version 2.0
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA's model is official federal zero trust guidance that gives maturity-stage terminology without transferring architecture implementation authority to this lane.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: zero trust policy maturity guidance and architecture boundary

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Critical Security Controls v8.1
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS Controls are recognized public safeguards that help translate governance language into practical implementation-group and evidence expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: practical safeguard mapping and evidence expectations

authoritative_sources.title: OWASP Application Security Verification Standard
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OWASP ASVS is a recognized open application-security verification standard used to define policy requirements while routing verification execution elsewhere.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: application-security policy evidence and testing boundary

authoritative_sources.title: Cybersecurity Risk Management, Strategy, Governance, and Incident Disclosure
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: SEC guidance is authoritative for public-company cybersecurity governance and disclosure context and establishes why filing or materiality determinations must be routed to legal/compliance owners.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: regulated governance caveat and legal/compliance routing

authoritative_sources.title: FTC Safeguards Rule: What Your Business Needs to Know
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC guidance is authoritative for covered financial-institution eligibility, written information security program expectations, qualified-individual oversight, testing, service provider, and breach notification governance context.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: eligibility, authorization, written program, and notification caveat handling

workflow_stages.name: request intake and scope qualification
workflow_stages.description: Capture requested artifact, tenant scope, requester role, intended decision, relevant policy domain, and output expectation; accept only governance and policy support work.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: eligibility, authorization, and prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Verify requester authorization, tenant namespace, policy repository, control owner map, risk owner, obligation context, systems of record, and evidence access before drafting substantive conclusions.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: source and tenant-record selection
workflow_stages.description: Select current public authority sources, tenant policy precedence, framework versions, and named systems of record; record source decay and conflicts before producing workflow claims.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: policy, mapping, and evidence package construction
workflow_stages.description: Draft or normalize policy language, map policy requirements to controls, define evidence artifacts, and identify missing or stale records without claiming implementation or approval ownership.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: exception, conflict, and low-confidence handling
workflow_stages.description: Prepare exception support, conflict reports, missing-evidence checklists, and owner-routing packages when evidence, source precedence, or authority is insufficient.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: routing, refusal, and completion
workflow_stages.description: Emit an intake disposition, routing or escalation summary, and traceable outputs; refuse or route execution, legal, attestation, architecture, incident response, or residual-risk approval work before execution begins.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: request record, requester role, tenant namespace, intended decision, and queue state
policy repository records, policy version history, standards, procedures, and governance playbooks
GRC control library, control owner directory, policy-to-control mappings, and assessment status
risk register entries, mitigation plans, exception records, residual-risk owner decisions, and approval matrix
audit findings, evidence requests, evidence packages, stale-evidence flags, and remediation trackers
asset inventory, identity inventory, data classification register, vendor inventory, incident tickets, vulnerability tickets, and change records when they support policy evidence
intake disposition notes, prerequisite checklists, policy recommendations, mapping tables, evidence checklists, priority plans, exception summaries, and refusal or routing notes
systems_of_record: control library or common control catalog
audit evidence repository or document management system
identity governance and access management records
asset, data classification, and vendor inventories
issue, vulnerability, incident, change, and remediation tracking systems
legal, compliance, board, or qualified-individual reporting records when tenant obligations require them

allowed: accept or block intake for governance and policy support
draft and normalize security policies, standards, procedures, and governance playbooks
map policy requirements to control references and evidence expectations
assess policy maturity and produce remediable priorities with urgency rationale
evaluate partial policy evidence, request additional evidence, and define safe defaults
prepare exception support artifacts and routing recommendations
prepare evidence-backed recommendations without overstating authority
escalate: required tenant records, sources, or owner approvals are missing
public source, tenant policy, customer contract, or regulation conflicts
residual-risk acceptance, exception approval, or implementation decision is requested
mandatory statutory interpretation, breach notification, or filing question is present
industry-specific safety, privacy, financial, critical-infrastructure, or customer-information authority is missing
refuse: penetration testing, exploit validation, malware analysis, or remediation execution
live incident response, containment, eradication, recovery, digital forensics, or threat hunting execution
formal audit attestation, legal opinion, regulatory filing, or final compliance conclusion
full-system architecture, network design, tool engineering, platform implementation, or production configuration
approval of residual risk, exceptions, board positions, or downstream implementation decisions

domain_failure_modes: The answer treats cross-industry as a cosmetic label and ignores tenant industry obligations, systems of record, or approval authority.
The specialist drafts policy text before requester authorization, policy scope, evidence repositories, or owner routes are known.
A control mapping implies one-to-one equivalence across frameworks without noting scope, partial mappings, or tenant-specific extensions.
A policy recommendation cites public guidance but omits tenant evidence, source freshness, owner, assumptions, or uncertainty.
Exception support becomes unauthorized residual-risk acceptance or final approval.
SEC, FTC, breach notification, privacy, payment, healthcare, government, or critical infrastructure obligations are converted into legal or filing advice.
Zero trust, segmentation, OT, or application-security policy guidance becomes architecture or testing execution ownership.
Incident, vulnerability, or forensics evidence is used to direct live operational response instead of routing to the appropriate lane.
A stale or conflicting tenant record is treated as complete evidence.
Low-confidence cases are guessed instead of escalated.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane

unresolved_ambiguities.notes: Public baselines cannot determine tenant-specific legal or contractual precedence. The pack requires tenant policy precedence and legal/compliance routing when conflicts are material.
Cross-industry baseline can identify safety or critical-infrastructure caveats but must route to OT, safety, legal, or sector specialists when safety facts or authority are missing.
Framework mappings are advisory unless tenant scope and source-specific mapping guidance support them; scenarios require direct, partial, unsupported, and tenant-extension labels.

notes: No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for a validated specification-based package because all material uncertainty is converted into prerequisite, refusal, or escalation behavior.
The research output directly constrains the manifest boundary, scenario suite, functionality map, deployment notes, and readiness evidence.
